what_manipulated: glucose availability (drink vs. none) and prior self-control exertion
what_measured: performance on executive-control tasks and blood glucose levels
experiment: Units performed varied self-control tasks (Stroop, thought suppression, emotion regulation, attention control). Blood glucose was measured before and after tasks. In separate tests, units performed an initial self-control task followed by a second self-control task; half received glucose supplementation, half did not. Analysis examined whether blood glucose levels mediated performance declines and whether glucose drink eliminated deficits after initial exertion.

what_manipulated: prior emotional regulation effort (suppression vs. control watching)
what_measured: performance on conflict-monitoring task and neural activity (ERN)
experiment: Units watched an emotional movie under instruction to suppress emotions or watch normally. Subsequently, units completed a Stroop task while electroencephalographic activity was recorded. The error-related negativity (ERN) waveform was measured to quantify conflict-monitoring system activation. Performance on Stroop and ERN amplitude were compared between conditions, with mediation analysis examining whether ERN weakness explained performance differences.

what_manipulated: prior self-regulation effort (emotional suppression, thought suppression, emotional control demands vs. none)
what_measured: subsequent performance on stamina task, puzzle-solving persistence, emotion control, and self-reported fatigue
experiment: Study 1: units regulated emotional response to movie, then physical stamina was measured. Study 2: units suppressed thoughts, then attempted unsolvable anagrams (persistence measured). Study 3: units suppressed thoughts, then controlled amusement response. Study 4: autobiographical retrospective accounts linked prior regulation demands and fatigue to subsequent regulatory failure. Analysis examined whether strength model fit better than alternative models (activation, priming, skill, constant capacity).

what_manipulated: prior executive-control effort (attention control, response inhibition, working memory load, emotional expression vs. none)
what_measured: subsequent performance on working memory and response inhibition tasks
experiment: Four experiments: Experiment 1, units controlled visual attention focus, then working memory span measured. Experiment 2, units inhibited writing tendencies, then reverse digit span tested. Experiment 3, units took working memory test, then subsequent working memory span tested. Experiment 4, units exaggerated emotional expressions, then response inhibition tested. Analysis examined whether control effort reduced subsequent performance and whether mood, motivation, or task difficulty explained effects.

what_manipulated: prior self-regulatory effort (attention regulation or emotion regulation vs. none)
what_measured: performance on complex thinking tasks (logic, reasoning, extrapolation, comprehension) and simple tasks (general knowledge, memorization)
experiment: Three studies: units either regulated attention (Studies 1, 3) or emotion (Study 2), then completed logic and reasoning test, cognitive extrapolation, or comprehension test. Control comparisons on general knowledge and memorization tasks examined whether depletion affected simple versus complex cognition. Analysis compared performance between depleted and control conditions across task complexity.

what_manipulated: harboring important secrets (secret condition vs. control, secret recency/preoccupation varied)
what_measured: perception and action related to physical effort (hill steepness, distance estimation, task effort prediction, helping behavior)
experiment: Four studies: units recalled, were preoccupied with, or suppressed important secrets. Units estimated hill steepness, perceived distance, predicted effort on physical tasks, and indicated willingness to help others with physical tasks. Correlational analysis examined relationship between burden and thought devotion to perception and action changes. Analysis compared secret and control conditions.

what_manipulated: money prime (money-related words or images vs. neutral reminders)
what_measured: social orientation and behavior (requests for help, helpfulness, work/play preference, physical distance)
experiment: Nine experiments: units were primed with money reminders or neutral concepts. Units then completed tasks assessing request for help, helping behavior, preference for solitary versus group work, and physical distance from others. Analysis compared preferences and behaviors between money-prime and neutral-prime conditions.

what_manipulated: blood glucose (sugar drink vs. artificial sweetener drink vs. control), with correlational variation in glucose levels
what_measured: delay discounting rate (preference for smaller-sooner vs. larger-later reward)
experiment: Units consumed either a sugar-containing drink, an artificial-sweetener drink, or control. Blood glucose was measured. Units made choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later monetary rewards. Correlation analysis examined relationship between blood glucose levels and discounting rates, controlling for sex, age, BMI, and taste. Analysis compared discount rates between drink conditions.

what_manipulated: distractor type (semantically related, phonologically related, or unrelated distractor pictures/words) and distractor modality (picture, word, or naming task)
what_measured: naming latency and gaze-shifting latency
experiment: Units named pictures with superimposed distractor pictures (semantically, phonologically, or unrelated) while eye movements were recorded (Experiment 1). Experiment 2 varied distractor presence and picture-word combinations. Experiment 3 compared picture naming and word reading with distractors. Analysis examined whether distractor effects on latency varied by semantic/phonological content, task type, and modality, and whether activation spread from concepts to phonological forms.

what_manipulated: list length, repetition presence, participant strategy instructions (guess vs. normal), and orthographic similarity (identical vs. similar items)
what_measured: repetition detection accuracy (repetition blindness or advantage)
experiment: Six experiments using rapid serial visual presentation: Experiments 1-2 examined basic RB/advantage with strategy instructions; Experiments 3-4 made guessing detectable by including similar but non-identical items; Experiments 5-6 removed identical items entirely. Units reported whether repetitions were detected. Analysis compared repetition accuracy across conditions to determine whether strategy and orthographic similarity accounted for RB versus advantage patterns.

what_manipulated: number of task switches in complex span task (varied switch frequency) and concurrent working memory load (item maintenance demand)
what_measured: recall performance on working memory task
experiment: Four experiments implemented task switching in continuous complex span tasks with controlled timing. Units performed sequences with varying numbers of task switches while maintaining items. Recall performance was measured. Analysis examined whether recall decreased as function of switch count and whether concurrent item maintenance load influenced switching cost, testing limited-resource model.

what_manipulated: retrieval practice (practiced vs. not practiced) and relearning (relearned vs. not relearned) and retrieval-practice block number
what_measured: final recall of studied pairs and relearning benefit magnitude
experiment: Units studied category-exemplar pairs, then underwent retrieval-practice blocks with relearning trials intermixed, then final recall test. Analysis examined whether retrieval-induced forgetting cumulated across blocks and whether relearned items showed accelerated relearning. Key comparison: whether prior forgetting enhanced relearning benefit (positive transfer) or not (negative transfer).

what_manipulated: stimulus presentation regime (intermixed vs. blocked exposure to similar stimulus pairs) and trial spacing
what_measured: discriminability of stimuli on test (discrimination accuracy)
experiment: Two experiments: units were exposed to similar stimulus pairs either intermixed (AX/BX trials alternated) or in separate blocks (CX then DX). Test examined whether intermixed exposure improved discriminability. Analysis compared discrimination accuracy across intermixed versus blocked conditions and examined whether spacing (separation between AX and BX presentations) explained the effect.

what_manipulated: studied items (old vs. new) and recognition status (hit, miss, correct rejection, false alarm)
what_measured: identification reaction time and recognition performance
experiment: Computational model simulations applied to continuous identification paradigm data. Model tested whether single system could account for: (a) priming without recognition, (b) RT patterns varying by recognition status, (c) fluency and priming in amnesic patients despite impaired recognition. Analysis compared model predictions to empirical patterns via simulation.

what_manipulated: item properties (phonological similarity, word length) producing effect-size variation
what_measured: recall accuracy and distribution of effect sizes
experiment: Feature model was tested against empirical distributions of immediate memory effects. Analysis examined whether model produced appropriate distributions of phonological confusion and word-length effects, including number of reversals (cases where similar or long items improved accuracy). Model was evaluated on whether it predicted reduced effects for lower-performing units without assuming encoding/rehearsal strategy changes.

what_manipulated: stereotype activation at retrieval (doctor/lawyer stereotypes assigned to study sources) and source labels provided at test
what_measured: source attribution accuracy and recollection reports (remember vs. know)
experiment: Units studied statements presented by two different people, labeled during study without stereotypes. At test, one source was labeled as a doctor and the other as a lawyer. Units attributed test items to sources and reported whether they "remembered" specific details. Analysis compared stereotype-consistent versus stereotype-inconsistent attributions and examined recollection reports. Three experiments tested whether expectancy-driven illusory recollections explained results.

what_manipulated: numerical notation (same vs. different Arabic/Indian notation pairs) and comparison dimension (physical vs. numerical)
what_measured: comparison latency and accuracy
experiment: Arabic speakers (familiar with Arabic and Indian numerical notations) compared digit pairs on physical (size) or numerical dimension. Two paradigms used: size comparison (Experiment 1) and same-different matching (Experiment 2). Analysis examined whether irrelevant numerical dimension was automatically processed even when notation differed, indicating cross-notation automatic translation to common magnitude representation.

what_manipulated: semantic neighborhood density (near vs. distant semantic neighbors) and neighborhood proximity
what_measured: word processing latency
experiment: Two experiments examined semantic neighborhood effects on visual word processing. Experiment 1 validated feature-based semantic representations and neighborhood measures. Experiment 2 measured word processing latency for words with dense near neighborhoods, dense distant neighborhoods, or sparse neighborhoods. Computational modeling examined whether attractor dynamics explained opposite effects (slowdown for dense near, speedup for dense distant neighbors).

what_manipulated: phonological similarity between to-be-recalled items and irrelevant sound stream (similar vs. dissimilar) and recall format (serial vs. free)
what_measured: recall accuracy and error types (intrusions)
experiment: Units recalled lists of phonologically related or unrelated items in serial or free format while hearing irrelevant sound streams with similar or dissimilar phonology. Analysis compared recall accuracy across recall formats and phonological conditions. Examined whether between-sequence similarity disrupted free but not serial recall and whether category-cueing processes explained format-dependent effects.

what_manipulated: operation practiced (multiplication vs. factoring vs. both) and whether same operation or reverse operation tested
what_measured: accuracy and reaction time on multiplication and factoring problems
experiment: Three experiments: Experiment 1, units practiced multiplication (6 x 9 = ?) then tested on factoring (54 = ? x ?). Experiment 2, units practiced factoring then tested on multiplication. Experiment 3, units practiced both operations then tested on both. Analysis examined whether transfer between operations was facilitative or interference-based as function of which operations were practiced.
